verdict.correction: The claim conflates two separate bugs/mechanisms. Correction: (1) The 400 errors in CHANGELOG around v2.1.136-v2.1.156 are caused by Claude Code storing thinking blocks with `thinking: ""` (empty) plus a `signature` field (the `display: "omitted"` behavior on Claude 4 models), then sending them back to the API whose signature-validation then fails because the signature was computed over the original non-empty thinking text. This means the local JSONL **does not contain the plain thinking text** for Claude 4 Opus/4.7/4.8 (which default to `display: "omitted"`) — it only stores an encrypted signature. (2) `redacted_thinking` is an entirely separate mechanism — it is a safety-system redaction that produces a distinct block type with a `data` field (no `thinking` or `signature`), and it **only applies to Claude 3.7 Sonnet, not Claude 4 models**. For a read-only inspector targeting 2026 Claude Code sessions (which use Claude 4): expect thinking blocks to have `thinking: ""` with a non-empty `signature` (unrecoverable text), not `redacted_thinking` blocks. Handle both defensively: if `block.type === "thinking" && block.thinking === "" && block.signature`, show a placeholder. If `block.type === "redacted_thinking"`, show "safety-redacted" UI. Both cases should be passed back unmodified to the API if constructing multi-turn requests.

corpus.summary: The read-only structured inspector can be built on top of the JSONL transcript as the single source of truth, augmented by a lightweight hooks side channel for push notifications. The host process tails the same JSONL file the running TUI session writes, parses it into typed events, and streams them over a second NWConnection multiplexed alongside the PTF byte stream. The client renders native SwiftUI views per event type. The design is substantially lower cost than a full B2 approach because it is purely observational: no slash commands, no model picker, no permissions UI, no duplicate-prompt risk. Subagent trees and agent-team inbox messages require extra JSONL path resolution (Happier's ClaudeRemoteSubagentFileCollector / claudeTeamInboxCollector patterns), and thinking blocks have a known gap where the thinking field is persisted as an empty string in Claude 4 models (load-bearing caveat).

points.claim: JSONL transcript is the canonical data source; the transcript_path field in every hook event gives the exact file path
points.detail: Every Claude Code hook event (SessionStart through SessionEnd — all 30+ event types) includes a common field `transcript_path` whose value is the absolute path of the session JSONL file, e.g. `/Users/username/.claude/projects/<hashed-project-id>/<session-uuid>.jsonl`. The SessionStart event additionally carries `session_id` and `source` (startup | resume | clear | compact). The host process should capture `transcript_path` from the SessionStart hook payload via stdin, then tail that exact file rather than relying on the `getProjectPath()` heuristic. Happier's `createSessionScanner` does exactly this: it accepts `transcriptPath` (from the hook) and `claudeConfigDir` overrides; the heuristic `getProjectPath(workingDirectory, claudeConfigDir)` = `join(claudeConfigDir, 'projects', resolveClaudeProjectId(workingDirectory))` is only a fallback when the hook path is unavailable. The file is append-only: Claude Code writes new newline-delimited JSON objects and never rewrites existing lines. Dedup by `uuid` field (present on all `user`, `assistant`, `system`, `progress` lines) is safe.
sources: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks (SessionStart input schema: session_id, transcript_path, source)

points.claim: JSONL record schema: five discriminated types — user, assistant, summary, system, progress — each with uuid for dedup, and .passthrough() for forward compatibility
points.detail: Happier's `RawJSONLinesSchema` (validated by Zod) defines the following discriminated union: (1) `user` — has `uuid`, `message.content` (string | any), optional `isSidechain: boolean`, `isMeta: boolean`; (2) `assistant` — has `uuid`, optional `message.usage`, `message.model`; message content array can contain `text`, `thinking`, and `tool_use` blocks; (3) `summary` — has `summary: string`, `leafUuid: string`; (4) `system` — has `uuid` (informational; Happier drops these wholesale); (5) `progress` — has optional `uuid`, fully passthrough. All types use `.passthrough()` so unknown fields survive without validation failure. The `message.content` array on assistant turns can include `{type:'thinking', thinking:'...', signature:'...'}` blocks interleaved with `{type:'text', ...}` and `{type:'tool_use', id, name, input}`. Tool results appear in subsequent `user` lines as `message.content` arrays of `{type:'tool_result', tool_use_id, content, is_error}`. Internal event types silently skipped: `file-history-snapshot`, `change`, `queue-operation` (Happy), plus all `system`-type lines (Happier). The `messageKey()` function uses `uuid` for user/assistant/system and `'progress:' + uuid` for progress records.

points.claim: Thinking/CoT blocks ARE present in the transcript but the thinking field is an empty string on Claude 4 models — a known upstream gap
points.detail: GitHub issue #31143 against anthropics/claude-code confirms: thinking blocks written to JSONL take the form `{"type":"thinking","thinking":"","signature":"EuUB..."}`. The `thinking` field is empty string; only the `signature` (encrypted hash) is populated. Claude Code discards the summarized thinking text before persistence. This is specific to Claude 4 (Opus 4.5+) which returns summarized rather than full thinking. The inspector can render a thinking card that shows the `signature` as proof-of-thinking and label it 'Thinking (content not persisted)'. Happier's `ThinkingTimelineRow` component (`apps/ui/sources/components/sessions/transcript/thinking/ThinkingTimelineRow.tsx`) renders a collapsible card with a `summary` prop and a `pulseEnabled` animation — in the live streaming case it receives thinking text via the ACP bridge, not from JSONL. The `thinkingText.ts` helper handles delta merging for live streams. For a purely JSONL-based inspector, the thinking content will be empty on Claude 4 unless Anthropic fixes issue #31143. Flag as load-bearing: if Anthropic restores the summarized thinking to JSONL (which the issue requests), the field will become non-empty and the inspector can display it without any code changes.
sources: https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/31143 (thinking field empty string, signature present, Claude 4 behavior)

points.claim: Subagent (Task) sidechains have their own JSONL files at a separate path under the session directory; the ClaudeRemoteSubagentFileCollector resolves and tails them
points.detail: When a subagent (Task tool) completes, the tool result in the parent JSONL includes an `output_file` path or a `tool_use_result.agentId`. The subagent's own conversation is stored at `<projectDir>/<sessionId>/subagents/agent-<agentId>.jsonl`. Happier's `resolveClaudeSubagentJsonlPath()` first tries the direct path, then scans subagent file headers for Agent Teams (display IDs like `Alpha@team` map to internal hashed IDs via first-line content matching with `youAreRe` / `summaryRe` regexes). `ClaudeRemoteSubagentFileCollector` observes every assistant message for `tool_use` blocks with `type=='tool_use'` and name matching generic subagent tools (`Task`, `Agent`), observes every user message for `tool_result` blocks, and when it sees a completed task it calls `registerTaskOutputFile()` which sets up a `JsonlFollower` (poll-based + `startFileWatcher`) on the sidechain JSONL. All ingested records are tagged `isSidechain: true` and `sidechainId: <tool_use_id>` so the client can group them under the parent tool call tree node. The sidechain ID is the `tool_use_id` of the spawning Task/Agent tool use.

points.claim: Agent Teams use a separate inbox mechanism at ~/.claude/teams/<teamName>/inboxes/team-lead.json — observable via claudeTeamInboxCollector
points.detail: Agent Teams teammates communicate with the team lead via a JSON inbox file at `<claudeConfigDir>/teams/<teamName>/inboxes/team-lead.json`. The `claudeTeamInboxCollector` watches this file (startFileWatcher), reads entries with `{from, text, timestamp, read}` shape, maps `from` (member name / member id) to the `tool_use_id` of the original Agent/Task spawning call, and emits synthetic `assistant` JSONL lines tagged `isSidechain:true, sidechainId:<toolUseId>` so they integrate into the subagent tree. The team name is extracted from `AgentTeamCreate`/`TeamCreate` tool use inputs or from `tool_use_result.team_name` when status is `teammate_spawned`. Entries are marked `read:true` after processing. This means Agent Teams messages are observable via the JSONL + inbox watcher pattern — no separate hook needed.

points.claim: Dynamic Workflows (research preview, Claude Code ≥ v2.1.154) run subagents in the background and are observable via SubagentStart/SubagentStop hooks and standard sidechain JSONL files; no special transcript format
points.detail: A Claude Code Workflow is a JavaScript orchestration script (stored in `.claude/workflows/` or `~/.claude/workflows/`) that the runtime executes outside the main conversation context. The runtime spawns subagents (up to 16 concurrent, 1000 total per run) which each write their own sidechain JSONL. The workflow itself is triggered by the `workflow` keyword in a prompt or by `/effort ultracode`. Observable data: (1) Each spawned worker subagent fires `SubagentStart` and `SubagentStop` hooks (carrying `agent_id`, `agent_type`, and the session-level `transcript_path`); (2) The worker's own JSONL sidechain is readable via the existing subagent JSONL resolution pattern; (3) The `/workflows` TUI command shows phase/agent progress in the TUI itself. The workflow runtime keeps intermediate results in script variables, not in Claude's context, so the main session JSONL may have sparse entries during a workflow run. The inspector can surface workflow runs by tracking SubagentStart/Stop hook events and matching them to sidechain files. The `disableWorkflows` setting and `CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_WORKFLOWS=1` env var can suppress the feature.
sources: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/workflows (full workflow spec, runtime constraints, /workflows command)

points.claim: Hooks as a supplementary push channel: SessionStart gives the transcript path with low latency; PostToolUse / SubagentStop give structured tool-name + input + output for immediate display without waiting for JSONL flush
points.detail: The host process can install hooks that fire to a local HTTP server or command. PostToolUse hook input includes `tool_name`, `tool_input` (full structured object), and `tool_output` (string). This provides sub-second push delivery of tool call data, compared to JSONL which has a file-watcher latency of ~100-500ms. SubagentStop provides `agent_id` and `agent_type`. SessionStart provides the authoritative `transcript_path` and `session_id`. Hooks do NOT include thinking/CoT content. Hooks fire in the claude process's own execution, so they share the same session. The inspector can use hooks as a low-latency event bus (tool call fired → show card immediately) and then backfill from JSONL once the line is flushed (for full output, diff content, etc.). The hook's `session_id` and `tool_input.tool_use_id` (when present) allow correlation with JSONL records by uuid.

points.claim: Data flow design: host tails JSONL + receives hooks via local HTTP → parses into typed events → multiplexes over a second NWConnection framed with length-prefixed JSON; client renders native SwiftUI views
points.detail: Recommended data flow: (1) Host spawns `claude` in PTF as today. (2) Host also registers a SessionStart hook that POSTs to a local HTTP listener (or writes to a named pipe) giving `session_id` + `transcript_path`. (3) Host's inspector daemon opens the JSONL file at `transcript_path` and starts a file watcher (FSEvents on macOS / kqueue). On each change it reads new lines, parses them, resolves subagent sidechain paths (ClaudeRemoteSubagentFileCollector pattern), and watches the team inbox path if agent teams are detected. (4) Optionally, host also registers PostToolUse / SubagentStart / SubagentStop hooks to an HTTP listener for low-latency structured push events. (5) Parsed events are serialized as length-prefixed JSON frames and sent over a second NWConnection (or a WebSocket) multiplexed on the same NetBird tunnel alongside the PTF byte stream — the PTF and inspector side channel are entirely independent byte streams, so there is no framing conflict. (6) Client side: NWConnection receives inspector frames, decodes them, and feeds a Swift actor that maintains an ordered event store. Views subscribe via Combine or async/await. On reconnect, client sends its last seen monotonic sequence number; host replays from that point in the event store (or re-reads JSONL from the beginning and re-emits from offset). Desktop: split-view with TUI on left and inspector panel on right. iOS: tab or sheet with timeline at top, tool-call detail on drill-down. READ-ONLY throughout — no input handling, no slash commands, no model picker needed on the inspector side.

points.claim: Views to render: tool-call cards, subagent tree, thinking blocks (empty-aware), todos/workflow panel, message timeline
points.detail: Based on the JSONL schema and Happier's UI components: (1) Tool-call card — rendered from an assistant `tool_use` block paired with the subsequent user `tool_result` block, linked by `tool_use_id`. Fields: tool name, structured input (pretty-printed JSON), output string, diff content (if Bash/Edit/Write), is_error flag, duration (derived from timestamps on assistant and user lines). (2) Subagent tree — each Task/Agent `tool_use` becomes a collapsible tree node; its sidechain JSONL events attach under it via `sidechainId == tool_use_id`; Agent Teams inbox messages also attach via the same `sidechainId`. Tree is collapsible to manage depth. (3) Thinking block — rendered inline within the assistant message timeline. Given the Claude 4 empty-string gap, show a 'Thinking (content unavailable)' placeholder with the signature prefix as a fingerprint, or show full text when non-empty (Claude 3 sessions or if Anthropic restores it). Happier's `ThinkingTimelineRow` provides a reference implementation. (4) Todos — Claude Code writes todo lists via the TodoWrite/TodoRead tools; the inspector can render the most recent `tool_result` content for tool_name == 'TodoRead' / 'TodoWrite' as a checklist panel. (5) Workflow panel — tracks SubagentStart/Stop hook events grouped by workflow run, shows phase/agent count and per-agent status. (6) Message timeline — chronological list of all parsed events with type icons (user prompt, assistant text, tool call, thinking, summary, progress).
sources: happier-dev/happier: apps/cli/src/backends/claude/types.ts (tool_use / tool_result block shapes via passthrough)

points.claim: Sync / dedup strategy: uuid-keyed processedMessageKeys set with monotonic sequence; replay on reconnect by re-reading JSONL from byte offset or full re-parse
points.detail: Happier's sessionScanner maintains `processedMessageKeys` (a Set<string>) keyed as: `uuid` for user/assistant/system, `'summary: '+leafUuid+': '+summary` for summaries, `'progress:'+uuid` for progress, `'sidechain:'+sidechainId+':'+uuid` for sidechain imports, `'team-inbox:'+sidechainId+':'+uuid` for team inbox. This is safe because JSONL is append-only and each record's uuid is stable. The scanner also runs a 3-second periodic poll (`setInterval`) as a safety net beside the FSEvents/kqueue file watcher. For the inspector side channel: the host assigns a monotonically increasing sequence number to each emitted event. The client tracks its `lastSeq`. On reconnect the host replays from `lastSeq` by either re-reading the JSONL file from the beginning (cheap for sessions under ~10MB) or by maintaining an in-memory ring buffer of recent events. The JSONL file itself never changes (append-only), so there is no race condition on re-read. Partial writes are handled by JSON.parse error catching — if the last line is a partial write (claude crashed mid-line), the parser silently skips it; on the next file-change event the line will be complete.
sources: happier-dev/happier: apps/cli/src/backends/claude/utils/sessionScanner.ts (processedMessageKeys, messageKey(), 3s interval, silent JSON parse error handling)

points.claim: Low-cost vs B2 justification: read-only means zero slash command / model picker / permission reimplementation and no duplicate-prompt risk
points.detail: A full B2 approach (driving claude via Agent SDK on the same machine) would require: a separate claude process (separate session, not the same TUI session), --bare / settingSources flags to suppress system prompt pollution, a separate model picker, separate permission grants, and risk of duplicate prompts if both the TUI and B2 clients submit to the same session. The JSONL-tail inspector avoids all of this: it is a passive file reader plus an optional one-way hook receiver. There is no input path from the inspector to the claude process. The inspector cannot accidentally trigger tool use, send prompts, or modify session state. The only write operation is the hooks listener (HTTP server receiving POSTs from claude's hook system), which is read-only from claude's perspective. Platform fit: desktop uses a split-view SwiftUI panel alongside the libghostty TUI view; iOS uses a tab bar or bottom sheet that surfaces the timeline and tool-call detail. Both are purely display surfaces with no text input. Effort estimate: ~2-3 weeks for the host-side JSONL tailer + hook listener + framing layer; ~2-3 weeks for the SwiftUI inspector views (tool card, thinking collapsible, subagent tree, workflow panel); subagent tree with sidechain resolution adds ~1 week. Total: ~5-7 weeks for a full-featured first version.
sources: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks (read-only hook subscriber pattern — hooks fire from claude, not to claude)
happier-dev/happier: apps/cli/src/backends/claude/utils/sessionScanner.ts (pure read pattern, no writes to transcript)

points.claim: Pitfalls: transcript lag vs TUI, large outputs truncation, sidechain ordering, and workflow runtime keeping intermediate results out of the main JSONL
points.detail: Four honest pitfalls: (1) Transcript lag: Claude Code flushes lines to JSONL as turns complete, not as text streams. The TUI shows streaming assistant text via the PTF byte stream while the JSONL line arrives only after the full turn is written. The inspector will show tool-call cards with a delay relative to the TUI. Mitigating approach: use PostToolUse hook for immediate display, then update with JSONL data when available. (2) Large tool outputs: JSONL lines for tool_result can be very large (e.g., grep results, file contents). The inspector should truncate display at ~50KB with a 'show more' expander and never block the main thread on large line parsing. (3) Sidechain ordering: sidechain JSONL events arrive via file watchers asynchronously and may interleave with parent session events in timestamp order. The inspector must sort by timestamp within each sidechain level and display them grouped under their parent tool_use node rather than in global chronological order. (4) Workflow intermediate results: the dynamic workflow runtime keeps intermediate phase results in script variables, not in the main session JSONL. The main JSONL may have a long gap during a workflow run (no assistant lines). The inspector should detect this via SubagentStart/Stop hook events and show a 'workflow running' state rather than appearing stuck. The workflow's subagents each have their own sidechain JONSLs accessible via the subagent resolution path.

corpus.project_relevance: This directly designs the read-only structured inspector for the native-Swift remote-coding tool's libghostty TUI setup. Every point maps to a concrete implementation decision: data source (JSONL), path acquisition (SessionStart hook transcript_path field), dedup strategy (uuid-keyed processedMessageKeys), subagent tree (ClaudeRemoteSubagentFileCollector pattern), thinking blocks (empty-string gap on Claude 4), workflow observability (SubagentStart/Stop hooks + sidechain JSONL), agent teams inbox (claudeTeamInboxCollector pattern), transport (second NWConnection over NetBird), and platform fit (desktop split-view, iOS tab/sheet).
claims_to_verify: Thinking blocks: the empty-string behavior on Claude 4 models needs verification against the current Claude Code version (the GitHub issue #31143 may have been fixed since it was filed; check the issue for resolution status)
Dynamic Workflows requiring Claude Code v2.1.154+: verify the current stable release version and whether workflows are now in GA vs still research preview

SubagentStop hook carrying agent_transcript_path field: the main hooks doc showed agent_id and agent_type but not agent_transcript_path explicitly — the web search summary mentioned it but the fetched doc schema did not include it; verify against current hooks reference
Subagent JSONL path pattern (<projectDir>/<sessionId>/subagents/agent-<agentId>.jsonl): this comes from Happier source, not the official Anthropic docs; it may change across Claude Code versions
Team inbox path (~/.claude/teams/<teamName>/inboxes/team-lead.json): same caveat — undocumented internal path from Happier source code, not in official docs
open_questions: Does the workflow runtime write any structured state file (e.g. a workflow-run JSONL or JSON) that the inspector could tail directly, or is the only observability path via SubagentStart/Stop hooks plus individual subagent JSONL sidechains?
Is there a TodoRead/TodoWrite JSONL entry format documented or standardized, or does the inspector need to parse tool_result content strings for todo state?

For the second NWConnection side channel: should it be a plain TCP socket over the NetBird WireGuard tunnel with a custom length-prefixed framing, or is a WebSocket server on the host more appropriate given existing tooling? What is the expected event rate (events/sec) for a busy workflow run with 16 concurrent agents?

verifications.claim: Thinking blocks: the empty-string behavior on Claude 4 models needs verification against the current Claude Code version (the GitHub issue #31143 may have been fixed since it was filed; check the issue for resolution status)

verdict.claim: Thinking blocks: the empty-string behavior on Claude 4 models needs verification against the current Claude Code version (the GitHub issue #31143 may have been fixed since it was filed; check the issue for resolution status)
verdict.evidence:
Issue #31143 ("[FEATURE] Persist summarized thinking text to session JSONL") is **Closed**, but NOT because Anthropic fixed the empty-string behavior. The resolution was a policy/opt-in change, not a code fix persisting thinking text by default.

**What actually happened (timeline from CHANGELOG and open issues):**

- **v2.1.69 (March 5 2026):** Anthropic silently introduced a server-side flag (`tengu_quiet_hollow`) that redacted all thinking text from JSONL before persisting. Thinking blocks appear in transcript as `{"type":"thinking","thinking":"","signature":"EuUBCk..."}`. This was undocumented in release notes (confirmed by issue #30958, which remains **Open**).

- **Workaround discovered:** Setting `"showThinkingSummaries": true` in `~/.claude/settings.json` bypasses the redaction (per issue #32997). When enabled, the summarized thinking text is again persisted to JSONL.

- **CHANGELOG v2.1.90 (April 2026):** Anthropic formally documented this as an intentional "Changed" behavior: *"Changed thinking summaries to no longer be generated by default in interactive sessions — set `showThinkingSummaries: true` in settings.json to restore."*  The default remains **empty string** without this setting.

- **Additional related fixes (not about JSONL persistence):**
  - v2.1.141: Fixed API error (400) when extended thinking emitted a redacted thinking block after a tool call.
  - v2.1.152: Fixed sessions stuck after model/login switch left stale thinking-block signatures in history.
  - v2.1.156: Fixed Opus 4.8 where thinking blocks were modified, leading to API errors.

- **Issue #63147 (filed May 28 2026, still Open as of June 2026):** Sessions with extended thinking + tool calls are permanently unrecoverable — the JSONL stores `"thinking": ""` with the original signature; when the session rebuilds from transcript, the API rejects it with 400 "thinking blocks cannot be modified" because the signature was computed over non-empty text. No fix deployed.

- **Issue #32810 (Closed as not planned):** Detailed empirical evidence (307 vs 112 blocks empty regression from v2.1.71→v2.1.72) — closed as not planned.

**Bottom line:** The empty-string behavior on Claude 4 models is **by design as of the current version**. The default is to store `"thinking": ""` in JSONL. Thinking text is only persisted to JSONL when `showThinkingSummaries: true` is set. Issue #31143 is closed but not because the empty-string default was fixed — it was closed after Anthropic formalized the opt-in workaround. The underlying problem of empty-string thinking blocks breaking session resume (via signature mismatch) remains an active open bug (#63147).
verdict.correction:
The claim that issue #31143 "may have been fixed" is **refuted**. The issue is closed but the empty-string behavior is the **current default** in all Claude Code versions. The correct characterization is:

1. **Default behavior (no setting):** `"thinking": ""` — thinking text is NOT persisted to JSONL. This is intentional as of v2.1.69+ and formalized in v2.1.90 CHANGELOG.

2. **Opt-in behavior:** Set `"showThinkingSummaries": true` in `~/.claude/settings.json` → summarized thinking text IS persisted to JSONL for Claude 4 models (which return a human-readable summary in the `thinking` field, not the raw encrypted CoT).

3. **Still-broken secondary consequence:** Even with `showThinkingSummaries: false` (default), the JSONL stores thinking blocks with `"thinking": ""` but retains the original `signature`. When Claude Code rebuilds the conversation from this transcript for session resume, it sends the empty-text + original-signature block to the API, which fails validation with 400 "thinking blocks cannot be modified." This is an **active unfixed bug** as of June 2026 (issue #63147, opened May 28 2026, still open).

**Implication for the read-only inspector design:** An inspector tailing the JSONL will see `"thinking": ""` for thinking blocks unless the target Claude Code process was started with `showThinkingSummaries: true`. The inspector cannot recover the thinking text from the JSONL alone in the default configuration. If thinking content is a required feature, the host must either: (a) require users to set `showThinkingSummaries: true`, or (b) intercept the streaming API response directly (not available from JSONL tail).

verifications.claim: Dynamic Workflows requiring Claude Code v2.1.154+: verify the current stable release version and whether workflows are now in GA vs still research preview

verdict.claim: Dynamic Workflows require Claude Code v2.1.154+
verdict.evidence:
The official Claude Code docs page at code.claude.com/docs/en/workflows states explicitly in a Note component: "Dynamic workflows are in research preview. They require Claude Code v2.1.154 or later and are available on all paid plans, with Anthropic API access, and on Amazon Bedrock, Google Cloud Vertex AI, and Microsoft Foundry."

npm registry confirms v2.1.154 was published on 2026-05-28T15:40:50 UTC, the same day as the Anthropic blog announcement (May 28, 2026). The changelog confirms v2.1.154 introduced Dynamic Workflows alongside Opus 4.8 as the default model.

The current latest stable release is v2.1.159 (published 2026-05-31), so v2.1.154 is NOT the latest — it is the minimum floor. The feature ships in research preview (not GA). "Research preview" means it is enabled for Max, Team, and Enterprise plans (admin-enabled), and for Pro plans via opt-in toggle in /config. It is not GA/generally available without opt-in.
verdict.correction: The version floor claim (v2.1.154+) is confirmed accurate per official docs. Two important corrections to the broader claim as often stated: (1) Workflows are still in RESEARCH PREVIEW as of May 28–31 2026, not GA — Pro users must enable them via /config toggle, Enterprise requires admin enablement. (2) The current latest stable version is v2.1.159 (May 31 2026), not v2.1.154, so "v2.1.154+" should be understood as the minimum required floor, not the current version. The technical mechanism is also more specific than most summaries state: a dynamic workflow is a JavaScript orchestration script that Claude writes for your task, executed by a separate runtime (not by Claude itself), with intermediate results stored in script variables outside Claude's context window, up to 16 concurrent agents and 1,000 agents total per run. The /workflows command, /deep-research bundled workflow, and ultracode effort level (/effort ultracode) are the primary surfaces. Workflow-level observability via hooks: SubagentStart/SubagentStop fire per agent spawned by a workflow; TaskCreated/TaskCompleted track task lifecycle; no dedicated WorkflowStart/WorkflowStop hook event exists as of this version.

verifications.claim: SubagentStop hook carrying agent_transcript_path field: the main hooks doc showed agent_id and agent_type but not agent_transcript_path explicitly — the web search summary mentioned it but the fetched doc schema did not include it; verify against current hooks reference

verdict.claim: SubagentStop hook carries an `agent_transcript_path` field — the main hooks doc showed `agent_id` and `agent_type` but not `agent_transcript_path` explicitly; the web search summary mentioned it but the fetched doc schema did not include it.
verdict.evidence: The claim is CONFIRMED: `agent_transcript_path` IS a real field in the SubagentStop hook input. The adversarial doubt was correct that the official shell-command hooks reference page (`code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks`) does not list it explicitly in a SubagentStop schema table — it only lists the common fields (`session_id`, `transcript_path`, `cwd`, `hook_event_name`, `permission_mode`) plus subagent context fields (`agent_id`, `agent_type`). However, the Agent SDK hooks page (`code.claude.com/docs/en/agent-sdk/hooks`) contains a live code example that explicitly accesses `input_data['agent_transcript_path']` in a Python SubagentStop callback, and the TypeScript example accesses `subInput.agent_transcript_path` on a `SubagentStopHookInput` typed object. Real production hook code in `disler/claude-code-hooks-multi-agent-observability` (`.claude/hooks/subagent_stop.py` line 100) reads `agent_transcript_path = input_data.get("agent_transcript_path", "")` and logs it. GitHub issue #7881 (from Sept 2025, when the field did NOT exist) shows the original SubagentStop only had `session_id`, `transcript_path`, and `stop_hook_active` — confirming the field was added AFTER that issue was filed, in response to requests like issue #16424. The field is now present in the hook payload but not yet surfaced in the shell-command hooks reference table, which is the documentation gap that caused the ambiguity.
sources: https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/16424 (feature request that drove the field addition)
https://code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks (shell-command hooks reference — agent_transcript_path absent from table, only in prose)
verdict.correction: The documentation discrepancy is real but the field EXISTS. The shell-command hooks reference page omits `agent_transcript_path` from the SubagentStop common fields table, while the Agent SDK hooks guide documents it via code example. The correct SubagentStop input schema is: `{ session_id, transcript_path, cwd, hook_event_name, permission_mode, effort.level, agent_id, agent_type, agent_transcript_path, stop_hook_active }`. Note: `transcript_path` (the PARENT session transcript) and `agent_transcript_path` (the SUBAGENT's own transcript, a separate JSONL file) are BOTH present and DIFFERENT paths.
